title: Pega Platform '25 — Rules Management
source: Pega Platform '25 Rules Management Official Documentation (256 pages)

topics.content: A Rule is the basic building block of a Pega Platform application that defines its behavior. Rules define the display of forms, UI fields, flows for work processes, and multiple other software elements. Rules are reusable — for example, a UI element to capture an address can be reused for both mailing and billing addresses. You configure Rules to create business solutions. Rules provide flexibility during development and help design applications efficiently for reuse in future projects. Key Rule concepts: each Rule has a unique identity combining Rule Type, name, class, and Ruleset. Rules are organized into Rulesets (for versioning and sharing) and classes (for inheritance and resolution). To create a Rule: Dev Studio > Create > select record category and type > enter label (best practice: up to 30 characters) > select Branch, Application Layer, Apply to class, and Ruleset > Create and open.

Rulesets provide a simple way to store, manage, and identify Rules that can be shared between applications. Instead of reusing individual Rules or creating new ones, you can reuse an entire Ruleset. Ruleset versioning format: MM-mm-pp (e.g., 01-01-01 default). The first two digits = major version (significant changes, new features), middle two = minor version (enhancements), last two = patch version (fixes). Ruleset categories: Standard (default, contains all Rule types), Component (designed for reuse across multiple systems), Shared (restricted, operates only on top-level pages). You can copy Rules between versions using 'skimming'. The Ruleset Maintenance wizard simplifies versioning, moving, and merging Rulesets. Important: never use 'Pega' or 'Pega-' as prefix for custom Ruleset names — these are restricted to Pega Platform. Create Rulesets via Dev Studio > Create > SysAdmin > Ruleset.

The Security tab on the Ruleset form controls who can change Rules and prevents conflicts when multiple users edit simultaneously. Key security features: Local customization flag (advisory, indicates Ruleset not intended for migration), Use check-out option (best practice to prevent overwriting — enables checkout/checkin workflow), Flow Rule for check-in approval (useful for large teams where a lead reviews changes before check-in). Password protection: To Add a Ruleset Version (password required before creating new versions), To Update a Ruleset (password to modify Ruleset properties). Locking Rulesets preserves them in their original state — locked versions cannot be modified. The Ruleset Maintenance wizard respects locks: copy operations allow locked sources but require unlocked targets; move operations require both source and target to be unlocked.

Classes organize Rules hierarchically and enable inheritance. Class hierarchy flows from general (@baseclass) to specialized classes. Classes use two inheritance mechanisms: Directed inheritance (explicit parent class specified) and Pattern inheritance (based on class name hyphens — e.g., Work-Contract-Application-Complete searches Work-Contract-Application-, Work-Contract-, Work-, etc.). Key class configuration: Keys array defines unique identity for class instances; Allow Locking controls database locking behavior; class-to-database-table mapping connects classes to storage. Auto-generated classes include Rule-DataObject (created with Data Objects), Data-AppMetaData (application metadata), and parallel History-classes. Class names have a maximum of 56 characters (64 with auto-generated History- prefix). The Delete a Class wizard helps safely remove classes by checking references.

Rule resolution is the process by which Pega Platform selects the most appropriate Rule when multiple definitions exist in different classes or Rulesets. The algorithm steps: 1) Identify all candidate Rules with matching purpose (key parts). 2) Assemble a temporary list of candidates. 3) Discard unavailable Rules. 4) Discard Rules from inapplicable Rulesets/versions (not in user's Ruleset list). 5) Rank remaining Rules by: Class (most specific wins), Ruleset (higher in list wins), Circumstance, Circumstance date, Version. 6) Select the first/best match. Factors influencing resolution: class hierarchy position, Ruleset list order, Access Roles and privileges, security/access control Rules, Rule availability status (Available/Blocked/Final/Withdrawn), and circumstance property values. Rule resolution applies to Rule Types inheriting from the abstract Rule- base class (Case Types, Properties, Data Pages, etc.) but NOT to data instances.

A completed Ruleset list contains sublists arranged in layers that determine Rule resolution order. Layer 1: Personal Ruleset — holds checked-out Rules, visible only to the operator, automatically created at login. Layer 2: Localized Rulesets — locale-specific Rulesets (e.g., _it_IT for Italian, _fr_CA for French Canadian), typically containing field values. Layer 3: Mobilized Rulesets — mobile-specific Rulesets with _mobile suffix. Layer 4: Production Rulesets — versions from the Production Rulesets array on the Access Group form. Layer 5: Application (current) — Rulesets from the Application Rule form, including Branch Rulesets displayed above the Ruleset they branched from, plus shared/component Rulesets. Layer 6: Application (built-on) — Rulesets for referenced built-on applications. Layer 7: Pega- Rulesets — system Rulesets. Layer 8: Pega-RULES — foundation Rulesets. Order is significant at every level.

Pattern inheritance searches superclasses based on prefixes of the class name ending in a hyphen. For class WalCare-Financial-Credit, the system searches: 1) WalCare-Financial-Credit, 2) WalCare-Financial-, 3) WalCare-Financial, 4) WalCare-, 5) WalCare. If no match found through all patterns, it follows directed inheritance to the parent class and repeats. This enables sharing general Rules at higher levels while overriding with specific Rules at lower levels. You can control pattern inheritance per class via the 'Find by name first (Pattern)?' checkbox on the class form. Classes using pattern inheritance can coexist with directed inheritance — if pattern inheritance is selected and the Rule isn't found via patterns, the system falls back to the directed parent class.

Rule availability controls how Rules participate in resolution at runtime. Statuses: Available — the Rule can be selected during resolution (default). Blocked — the Rule and all similar Rules (same name/key, any Ruleset) cannot be executed; blocked Rules are not removed during skimming. Final — the Rule is used during resolution but cannot be extended, overridden, or modified; prevents creating second Rules with same version and same or lower Ruleset version. Withdrawn — communicates that the Rule is no longer supported. Deprecated — indicates Pega Platform no longer supports the Rule. Extension — enables users to override the Rule for custom functionality (extension points). Template — allows users to use this Rule as a starting point. When availability changes to Withdrawn or Blocked, developers can document justification and alternate Rules via the History tab.

Circumstancing creates Rule variants triggered only under specified conditions without changing core logic. Three circumstancing methods: 1) By Property — evaluate a property value at runtime (e.g., LoanAmount > 5000). 2) By Date — specify start/end dates for time-limited Rules; if multiple date-circumstanced Rules are candidates, the one with the nearest end date is selected, then most recent start date. 3) By Property and Date — combine both. Circumstance templates group related properties, and circumstance definitions store values to evaluate. Redirected Rules: circumstance Rules that reference another circumstance Rule's logic — reduces maintenance when multiple circumstances behave identically (e.g., multiple states with same tax rate). Base Rules are the fallback selected when no circumstance version's criteria match. Use Data-Circumstance-Duplicates report to identify conflicts.

Branches support parallel development by providing isolated workspaces for changes. Each Branch has its own Rulesets used for Rule resolution. Branch Rulesets display above the Ruleset they branched from in the Ruleset list. When creating a Rule, you can select a Branch to store it in — if the branched Ruleset doesn't exist, it's automatically created. Withdrawn Rules in Branch Rulesets are an exception: they are withdrawn only in the Branch Ruleset, not from the main Ruleset during development. When you move changes to the target (merge), the withdrawn Rule takes effect. Checked-out Rules in Branches follow the same checkout/checkin workflow as regular Rulesets. When working with the Ruleset Maintenance wizard and Branches, ensure all Rules are checked in before operations.

The checkout/checkin system prevents conflicts when multiple developers work on the same Rules. Checkout modes: Standard checkout — locks the Rule for editing, other developers see the previous version. Private edit — allows editing without locking. Branch checkout — checks out to a specific Branch Ruleset. When checked out, Rules go to your Personal Ruleset at the top of the Ruleset list, visible only to you. Checkin process: can include an approval flow managed by a flow Rule on the Ruleset Security tab — useful for large teams where a lead reviews changes. Bulk checkin is supported via Configure > Application > Development > Checked Out Rules. Tips: always check in Rules before running the Ruleset Maintenance wizard; use the @baseclass.ToolbarFull or @baseclass.ToolbarSaveAs privilege for copy operations.

Skimming copies Rules from a Ruleset into a higher Ruleset version, collecting only the highest version of every Rule. This is used when creating new major/minor versions of an application. During skimming: Blocked Rules are NOT removed (they persist to block similar Rules regardless of Ruleset name). Circumstanced Rules can be skimmed — if you want a skimmed Rule to be recognized as a base Rule, select the Base Rule checkbox. The Ruleset Maintenance wizard supports skimming through its merge operation — you can compress Rulesets down to a lower level (01-01-01) or designated level, controlling the merge by specifying target version and processing order. Before skimming/merging: back up your system, ensure all Rules are unlocked and checked in, and review prerequisites.

Adjust Rule resolution by moving Rules between Rulesets and classes. When moving a Rule, all old instances are removed and new instances are created in the target location — the pzInsKey does not change. When copying, original Rules remain and new copies are created. Access via Dev Studio: App > Classes tab > right-click class > Refactor > Move Rules > specify destination class, Ruleset, and version. Constraints: moved Rules must not have the same class, keys, and Ruleset version as existing Rules. The Ruleset Maintenance wizard also supports moving and merging entire Rulesets/versions — it updates references in Application Rules, Access Groups, Access Roles, Connect Rules, Declare Pages, and many other Rule types that reference the affected Rulesets.

Best practices for naming records: Use descriptive, informative names promoting reuse. Start names with letters, use only letters, numbers, and hyphens. Labels auto-generate identifiers (spaces/special chars removed, CamelCase). Active Rules (activities, flows) start with a verb indicating main action plus a noun. Passive/object Rules (properties, roles) use nouns or noun phrases. Specific conventions: Access Groups use ApplicationName:RoleName format (e.g., BakingOperations:Customer). Data Pages start with D_. Flows use verb + noun (e.g., ProcessClaim). Router activities start with 'To' (e.g., ToWorkbasket). Database tables use LOB name_work_workpool format. Constraints use table name + _PK suffix. File Rules use lowercase only. Class names max 56 characters. Property names max 64 characters. Labels max 64 characters (best practice: 30). System name max 32 characters.

The Ruleset Maintenance wizard simplifies Ruleset operations: Moving (move all Rules from source to target Ruleset, updating references), Copying (duplicate Rules to a new Ruleset/version), Merging (combine multiple source versions — skim highest version of each Rule, compress to target level), and Change a Version (update version numbers). The wizard depends on Pega-RuleRefactoring agents — ensure they're enabled. It automatically updates references in: Rule-Application, Rule-Access-Role-Name, Rule-Connect-SOAP, Rule-Declare-Pages, Data-Admin-Operator-ID, Data-Admin-Connect-EmailListener, and many more. Recovery: before merging, the tool creates backups (Bkup_SourceRuleSet.zip, Bkup_TargetRuleSet.zip) that can be restored via Import. Each execution logs to Log-Merge-RuleSet class. Prerequisites: back up system, ensure Rules are unlocked and checked in, confirm full-text search is enabled for faster processing.

Production Rulesets are listed in the Production Rulesets array on the Access Group form's Layout tab. They appear in Layer 4 of the Ruleset list, above the Application Rulesets. Production Rulesets allow operators to have access to additional Rules beyond those defined in the application — useful for production-specific configurations, customizations, or overrides that shouldn't be part of the standard application package. This separation ensures clean application architecture where production-specific adjustments don't mix with development Rulesets.

Ruleset validation ensures that Rules referenced by an application are available on the target system. Two modes: Application Validation (AV) mode — validates the Ruleset within the application context only; if you want to use an AV-mode Ruleset in more than one application, first create an additional Application Layer and use it as a built-on application. Ruleset Validation (RV) mode — validates based on prerequisites defined in another Ruleset. Validation does not affect Rule resolution at runtime. Configure via the Ruleset version settings: Edit Ruleset form > Validation mode section. The validation tool can be used to check Rule integrity. Always configure validation when setting up Rulesets to ensure deployment reliability.

Extension points are activities or other Rules designed to be overridden for application-specific needs. Many are empty stubs containing no steps. Typically, the overriding Rule is in a derived class. Pega Platform includes dozens of built-in extension points — for example, Code-Security.ApplicationProfileSetup executes at operator login for custom initialization. Extension Rules have their availability set to 'Extension' to signal they're meant to be overridden. Base Rules are the fallback selected by Rule resolution when no circumstance version matches. When you skim circumstanced Rules to a higher version, you can select the 'Base Rule' checkbox so the skimmed Rule is recognized as a base Rule during resolution. This avoids having to copy all circumstanced variants to the higher version.

topics.topic: Rule History, Versions, and Generated Code
topics.content: Pega Platform tracks Rule changes through history and versioning. View Rule history via the History tab on any Rule form — shows who changed the Rule, when, and why. View Rule versions to see all versions of a Rule across Rulesets. View generated Java code to understand the compiled output of a Rule. View XML representation of a Rule for debugging or export purposes. Replace strings across Rulesets using the string replacement utility. Find deprecated Rules in sections to identify outdated references. These tools support governance, auditing, and maintenance of large applications with many Rules across multiple Rulesets and versions.

Pega Platform supports Unicode characters in Rule names including multibyte characters. The EnableUnicodeRuleNames dynamic system setting (DSS) controls this feature — default is disabled, an administrator must enable it. App Studio supports Unicode in property names, Case Type names, and data type names. This feature is designed for Pega Cloud services — for local deployments, the application server and database must support UTF-8. Enabling Unicode allows international development teams to use their native language characters in Rule naming, improving readability and collaboration across global teams.

qa_pairs.question: What is Rule resolution in Pega and how does it work?
qa_pairs.answer: Rule resolution is the process Pega Platform uses to select the most appropriate Rule when multiple definitions exist across classes and Rulesets. The algorithm: 1) Identify all candidate Rules matching the purpose (key parts like Applies-to class and name). 2) Discard unavailable Rules and those from inapplicable Rulesets. 3) Rank remaining candidates by: Class (most specific wins via inheritance), Ruleset (higher in list wins), Circumstance, Circumstance date, and Version. 4) Select the first/best match. Factors: class hierarchy, Ruleset list order, Access Roles/privileges, Rule availability (Available/Blocked/Final/Withdrawn), and circumstance values. This enables sharing general Rules at base levels while overriding with specific Rules in derived classes.

qa_pairs.question: What are Rulesets in Pega and how is versioning structured?
qa_pairs.answer: Rulesets are containers that store, manage, and identify groups of Rules for sharing between applications. Version format is MM-mm-pp (e.g., 01-01-01): first two digits = major version (significant changes), middle two = minor version (enhancements), last two = patch (fixes). Categories: Standard (default, all Rule types), Component (reuse across systems), Shared (restricted, top-level pages only). Key operations: skimming (copying highest Rules to a new version), merging (combining multiple versions), moving, and copying — all managed through the Ruleset Maintenance wizard. Security features include password protection, checkout/checkin, and locking. Never prefix custom Rulesets with 'Pega' or 'Pega-'.

qa_pairs.question: How does pattern inheritance work in Pega Rule resolution?
qa_pairs.answer: Pattern inheritance searches for Rules by systematically truncating the class name at hyphens. For example, for class WalCare-Financial-Credit, the system searches: 1) WalCare-Financial-Credit, 2) WalCare-Financial-, 3) WalCare-Financial, 4) WalCare-, 5) WalCare. If no match is found through all patterns, the system follows directed inheritance to the explicitly specified parent class and repeats the pattern search there. This allows general Rules defined at higher class levels to be inherited by more specific classes, while specific Rules at lower levels override the general ones. Controlled via the 'Find by name first (Pattern)?' checkbox on the class form.

qa_pairs.question: What are the different Rule availability statuses in Pega?
qa_pairs.answer: Available — default, the Rule participates in resolution normally. Final — Rule is used in resolution but cannot be extended, overridden, or modified; no second Rule with same version and same/lower Ruleset version can be created. Blocked — the Rule AND all similar Rules (same name/key, any Ruleset) are blocked from execution; persists through skimming. Withdrawn — communicates the Rule is no longer supported. Deprecated — Pega Platform no longer supports the Rule. Extension — designed to be overridden for custom functionality. Template — available as a starting point for new Rules. When changing to Withdrawn or Blocked, developers can document justification and alternate Rules via the History tab.

qa_pairs.question: What is circumstancing in Pega and what methods are available?
qa_pairs.answer: Circumstancing creates Rule variants that trigger only under specified conditions. Three methods: 1) By Property — evaluates a property value at runtime (e.g., when LoanAmount > 5000, use the specialized Rule). 2) By Date — Rules active only during specified time periods (start/end dates); multiple date-circumstanced candidates are resolved by nearest end date, then most recent start date. 3) By Property and Date — combines both conditions. Redirected Rules reduce maintenance when multiple circumstances behave identically. Base Rules serve as fallback when no circumstance matches. Circumstance templates group properties, and definitions store evaluation values. Use the Data-Circumstance-Duplicates report to find conflicts.

qa_pairs.question: What are the layers in a Pega Ruleset list and their order?
qa_pairs.answer: The Ruleset list has 8 layers determining Rule resolution order: Layer 1 — Personal Ruleset (checked-out Rules, visible only to you). Layer 2 — Localized Rulesets (locale-specific, e.g., _it_IT). Layer 3 — Mobilized Rulesets (_mobile suffix). Layer 4 — Production Rulesets (from Access Group form). Layer 5 — Application current (from Application Rule, including Branch and component/shared Rulesets). Layer 6 — Application built-on (Rulesets from referenced built-on applications). Layer 7 — Pega- system Rulesets. Layer 8 — Pega-RULES foundation. Order is significant at every level — higher layers take precedence in Rule resolution.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Ruleset Maintenance wizard work?
qa_pairs.answer: The wizard supports four operations: Moving (transfers Rules from source to target Ruleset, deletes source), Copying (duplicates to new Ruleset/version), Merging (combines multiple versions, skims highest version of each Rule), and Change a Version. It depends on Pega-RuleRefactoring agents being enabled. Automatically updates references in Application Rules, Access Groups, Access Roles, Connect Rules, Declare Pages, operator IDs, and more. Creates backup files (Bkup_SourceRuleSet.zip, Bkup_TargetRuleSet.zip) before merging. Prerequisites: back up system, ensure Rules are unlocked and checked in, enable full-text search for faster processing. Each execution logs to Log-Merge-RuleSet.

qa_pairs.question: How does the checkout/checkin system work in Pega?
qa_pairs.answer: The checkout/checkin system prevents conflicts when multiple developers work on the same Rules. Checkout modes: Standard checkout (locks the Rule, others see previous version), Private edit (edit without locking), Branch checkout (checks out to a Branch Ruleset). Checked-out Rules go to your Personal Ruleset at the top of the Ruleset list, visible only to you. Checkin can include an approval flow via a flow Rule configured on the Ruleset Security tab — useful for large teams requiring lead review before changes go live. Bulk checkin is available via Configure > Application > Development > Checked Out Rules. Best practice: always use checkout to prevent overwriting, and check in all Rules before Ruleset maintenance operations.

qa_pairs.question: What are the naming conventions for Pega Rules and records?
qa_pairs.answer: Key naming conventions: Start names with letters, use letters/numbers/hyphens only. Labels auto-generate CamelCase identifiers. Active Rules (activities, flows) use verb + noun (e.g., ProcessClaim). Passive Rules (properties, roles) use nouns (e.g., ClaimID). Access Groups: ApplicationName:RoleName format (e.g., LoanRequests:Administrator). Data Pages start with D_. Router activities start with 'To' (e.g., ToWorkbasket). Database tables: LOBname_work_poolname. File Rules use lowercase. Size limits: class names max 56 chars, property names max 64 chars, labels max 64 chars (best practice: 30), system names max 32 chars, node names max 32 chars. Always check for existing Rules before creating new ones to promote reuse.

qa_pairs.question: What is Rule skimming in Pega and when should you use it?
qa_pairs.answer: Skimming copies only the highest version of each Rule from source Ruleset versions into a higher target version. Use it when creating new major/minor versions of an application to consolidate Rules. Key behaviors: Blocked Rules are NOT removed (they persist to block Rules in underlying Application Layers). Circumstanced Rules can be skimmed — use the Base Rule checkbox so skimmed Rules are recognized as base Rules during resolution. The Ruleset Maintenance wizard handles skimming through its merge operation. Before skimming: back up system, ensure all Rules are unlocked and checked in, and review prerequisites. Recovery is possible via backup ZIP files created automatically before the operation.
